topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, the subject line stark and unforgiving: "Event Cancellation." My hands started to tremble, the cheap plastic of my phone slick beneath my fingers. I reread the message three times, the words blurring into an incomprehensible mess. A coldness began to creep up my spine, settling deep in my chest like a heavy stone. I stared at the screen, the vibrant colors of the notification mocking the sudden, suffocating emptiness that had flooded my senses. Every muscle in my body tightened; I felt a desperate urge to bolt from the chair, to disappear into the nearest shadow. The silence of my apartment, usually comforting, now felt vast and hostile, amplifying the frantic drumming of my pulse against my ribs.

[story 2]

When Sarah finally called, her voice was strained, brittle. "They... they decided not to do it, Mark." The words hung in the air, sharp and cutting. I gripped the phone so hard my knuckles turned white, the plastic groaning under the pressure. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as if my lungs had forgotten how to draw a full breath. I looked around the room, at the scattered decorations that suddenly seemed garish and mocking. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I couldn't form coherent sentences; only a dry, rasping sound escaped my throat. I just wanted to run, to find a place where the sound of that phone call wouldn't echo in my head.

[story 3]

I sat frozen at the kitchen table, the printed invitation lying crumpled beside my coffee mug. The ink seemed to swim before my eyes. It wasn't a typo; it was definitive. A slow, creeping paralysis took hold. My jaw locked so tight it ached. I could feel a distinct tingling sensation radiating from my fingertips, a strange, prickly awareness that made the air feel thin. I kept glancing toward the front door, expecting someone to burst in, to shatter the reality of the situation. The quiet in the house was oppressive, heavy, pressing down on my shoulders until moving seemed like an impossible, monumental effort.
